headline: XVII. Tätigkeitsbericht 2003 / 2004 | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
# German Data Protection Commissioner Releases Biennial Activity Report for 2003-2004

Bavaria's State Data Protection Commissioner has published the 17th Activity Report covering 2003 and 2004, providing a condensed overview of key data protection developments and regulatory findings in a management summary format.

The report identifies future data protection policy and supervisory requirements while offering guidance on priority data protection issues. It marks a shift in how such reports are presented, using internet functionality to link report content directly to checklists, orientation guides, and supplementary reference materials — making detailed compliance information publicly accessible online.

The commissioner says the linked digital format allows authorities to deliver compact, up-to-date information on risks to the right of informational self-determination, alongside recommended protective measures and practical solutions.

The right to informational self-determination — a foundational principle in German data protection law established by the Federal Constitutional Court in 1983 — underpins the report's regulatory framing.

The report is accompanied by a dedicated press summary and is positioned not only as a regulatory accountability document but as an invitation for broader public debate on the current status of data protection in German society.

The publication signals growing institutional recognition that data protection oversight must extend beyond formal compliance channels to reach the general public through accessible digital tools.

headline: XVIII. Tätigkeitsbericht 2005 / 2006 | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
## German State Data Protection Commissioner Submits Biennial Report to Parliament

German State Data Protection Commissioner Wahlbrink submitted the 18th Activity Report, covering 2005 and 2006, to State Parliament President on October 25, 2007.

The report follows the condensed format introduced in the 2003/2004 edition, which received predominantly positive feedback. It documents significant developments in data protection law and key findings from the Commissioner's oversight work during the two-year period.

The report addresses risks to the right to informational self-determination — a constitutional principle in Germany — and outlines protective measures and solutions to identified problems. To improve public accessibility, the report's chapters are indexed and linked through summaries on the Commissioner's website, allowing citizens and stakeholders to navigate specific topics directly.

Commissioner Wahlbrink stated: "Please understand the 18th Activity Report again as an invitation for critical discussion about the current status of data protection in our society. I am looking forward to your reactions."

The submission continues the established practice of biennial reporting to the state legislature, providing parliamentary oversight of data protection compliance across state government activities. The online publication format reflects efforts to make regulatory findings more accessible to the public, moving beyond traditional printed reports to enable broader engagement with data protection issues affecting residents.

headline: Advisories || Business
body:
# Ghana's Cyber Security Authority Identifies 86 Fake Military Recruitment Accounts

Ghana's Cyber Security Authority (CSA) has identified 86 fraudulent social media accounts impersonating the Ghana Armed Forces (GAF) within a single week, all engaged in recruitment fraud targeting the public.

The surge follows the launch of GAF's 2025 enlistment and recruitment exercise on October 1, 2025. The fake accounts impersonate GAF personnel, officers, and high command figures, exploiting public interest in the official recruitment process to defraud victims.

The CSA is actively working with social media platforms to take down the identified profiles while conducting further investigations. The authority issued its advisory on October 27, 2025, under reference CSA/CERT/MPA/2025-10/01.

The CSA operates under the Cybersecurity Act, 2020 (Act 1038), which grants it authority to regulate cybersecurity activities and respond to cybercrime in Ghana.

Members of the public are urged to report suspected fraudulent accounts or seek guidance through the CSA's 24-hour reporting channels: phone or text at 292, WhatsApp at 0501603111, or email at report@csa.gov.gh.

The incident highlights a recurring pattern in which fraudsters exploit high-profile government recruitment periods to launch impersonation campaigns, targeting job seekers with false promises of military enlistment.

headline: Bipartisan Coalition Announces Settlement with Delaware in MoneyGram Case Over $190 Million in Unclaimed Property Litigation - Alabama Attorney General's Office
body:
# 35 States Reach $190M Settlement with Delaware Over MoneyGram Unclaimed Property

A bipartisan coalition of 35 states has settled a U.S. Supreme Court dispute with Delaware over unclaimed property from MoneyGram Payment Systems, securing more than $190 million for distribution to coalition states and their residents.

Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall announced the settlement August 29, resolving the damages phase of *State of Delaware v. Commonwealth of Pennsylvania et al.* and *State of Arkansas et al. v. State of Delaware*.

The dispute stemmed from Delaware advising MoneyGram to allocate unclaimed official checks to Delaware rather than to the states where those instruments were purchased. In 2023, a unanimous Supreme Court ruled the Federal Disposition Act governs such unclaimed official checks, siding with the multi-state coalition.

Under settlement terms, Delaware will transfer more than $102 million covering MoneyGram-reported property from 2011 to 2017, distributed based on each instrument's place of purchase. That transfer covers roughly half the disputed report years. Separately, approximately $89 million held in a litigation escrow account covering 2018 to 2022, plus accrued interest, will be distributed among all 50 states. Coalition states receive nearly $55 million from the escrow portion.

States assume custody of received funds and bear responsibility for returning property to rightful owners and paying valid claims.

Arkansas, Pennsylvania, California, Texas, and Wisconsin initiated the original Supreme Court action, joined by 29 additional states including Alabama.

headline: AG Strange Praises Unanimous U.S. Supreme Court Ruling As A Victory For Class Action Fairness and Free Enterprise - Alabama Attorney General's Office
body:
# Supreme Court Unanimously Rules Class Action Stipulation Cannot Block Federal Jurisdiction

The U.S. Supreme Court ruled unanimously on March 19, 2013, that plaintiffs in *Standard Fire Insurance Co. v. Knowles* cannot use pre-litigation damage stipulations to avoid federal court jurisdiction under the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005.

The case arose after plaintiffs filed a class action in an Arkansas state court with a history of favorable rulings for plaintiff attorneys. To keep the case out of federal court, plaintiffs' lawyers filed a stipulation promising not to seek damages exceeding $5 million — the CAFA threshold — even though actual claims potentially exceeded that amount.

The Court rejected the maneuver, ruling the case belongs in federal court.

Alabama Attorney General Luther Strange, who authored an amicus brief joined by 18 other states, argued the stipulation procedure would "subvert the interests of absent class members to the interests of the class's lawyers." The brief identified the procedure's primary purpose as circumventing federal reforms, including requirements that state officers receive notice and opportunity to challenge proposed settlements.

Strange called the ruling "a tremendously important victory for class action fairness and free enterprise," noting that abusive procedures in other states' courts directly harm Alabama businesses and consumers.

The 18 co-signing states included Arizona, Florida, Texas, Ohio, Michigan, and Georgia, signaling broad multistate concern about plaintiffs' attorneys using jurisdictional manipulation to avoid CAFA's consumer protections.

headline: Attorney General Marshall Announces Court of Criminal Appeals Upholds Capital Murder for Hire Conviction in Mobile County - Alabama Attorney General's Office
body:
# Alabama Appeals Court Upholds Capital Murder-for-Hire Conviction

Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall announced September 25, 2024 that the Alabama Court of Criminal Appeals affirmed the capital murder-for-hire conviction of Jason Christopher Hill, 48, of Ellabell, Georgia.

Hill was originally convicted September 1, 2023 in Mobile County Circuit Court and sentenced to life imprisonment without possibility of parole. He subsequently appealed seeking to have the conviction reversed.

Trial evidence showed Hill believed his wife had been raped by victim Brent Allen when she was thirteen years old. Hill hired a private investigator to locate Allen, then recruited his uncle to carry out the killing. Hill provided the hired killer with a silencer-equipped rifle, ammunition, concealment masks, and a bulletproof vest. The uncle shot and killed Allen outside his Satsuma, Alabama home on December 6, 2020. Investigators solved the case within ten days.

The Mobile County District Attorney's office under Keith Blackwood prosecuted the original case. During the appeals process, the Attorney General's Criminal Appeals Division defended the conviction before the appellate court. The Court of Criminal Appeals issued its affirmation decision September 20, 2024.

Attorney General Marshall credited Assistant Attorney General Christopher D. Howell for handling the appeal and acknowledged District Attorney Blackwood's office for assistance in defending the conviction.

headline: XIX. Tätigkeitsbericht 2007 / 2008 | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
## Lower Saxony Data Protection Chief Warns of Surveillance Gaps, Calls for Executive Action

Lower Saxony Data Protection Commissioner Joachim Wahlbrink presented his biennial report Friday in Hanover, identifying employee surveillance, Google Street View, and expanding video monitoring networks as the dominant privacy concerns of 2007-2008.

Wahlbrink highlighted fines totaling €1.46 million imposed nationally on a major discount food retailer for systematic employee surveillance — €656,000 of which came from Lower Saxony alone — and renewed calls for dedicated employee data protection legislation.

The commissioner raised particular concern about mobile phone tracking, warning that network cell data creates near-continuous location records. He noted a sharp rise in citizen inquiries about commercial services enabling covert location tracking of individuals, including partners and employees, without their knowledge. "The mobile phone effectively mutates into a motion detector, making positions available on an internet platform," Wahlbrink said.

Following data breaches at SchülerVZ, AWD, Postbank, Libri, and the Federal Employment Agency, Wahlbrink urged companies and public bodies to continuously audit their data security systems, warning that reputational damage from breaches should not be underestimated.

Wahlbrink also flagged an European Court of Justice case brought by the European Commission against Germany, alleging that state oversight of regional data protection authorities violates the EU Data Protection Directive's requirement for "complete independence" from government bodies including interior ministries.

The commissioner acknowledged a shortage of qualified IT personnel is hampering his office's ongoing corporate inspections.

headline: Attorney General Luther Strange Praises Healthcare Ruling - Alabama Attorney General's Office
body:
# Alabama Attorney General Praises Federal Court Ruling Striking Down Affordable Care Act

Alabama Attorney General Luther Strange praised a federal court ruling that struck down the 2010 Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act in its entirety, calling it a victory for constitutional federalism.

U.S. District Court Judge Roger Vinson, sitting in Pensacola, ruled January 31, 2011 that Congress exceeded its constitutional authority by enacting the law's individual mandate, which requires Americans to purchase health insurance. The ruling invalidated the entire statute, not just the mandate provision.

Alabama was among 26 states that joined the lawsuit challenging the law, along with the National Federation of Independent Business and two individual plaintiffs. Florida Attorney General Pam Bondi led the multi-state coalition before Judge Vinson.

Strange framed the ruling as a check on federal overreach. "Our Constitution says that the federal government is one of limited, enumerated powers," Strange said. "If Obamacare were upheld, then it would put us on a path where Congress could regulate each and every phase of our lives."

Strange confirmed Alabama will continue working with Florida and other plaintiff states as the case advances toward the U.S. Supreme Court.

The ruling centered on the Commerce Clause and whether Congress holds authority to compel individuals to enter into commercial transactions. The case represented one of the most significant legal challenges to the healthcare law at the time.

headline: Attorney General Marshall Hosts Law Enforcement Training on Campus Extremism and Antisemitism - Alabama Attorney General's Office
body:
# Alabama AG Hosts Law Enforcement Training on Campus Extremism and Antisemitism

Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall convened a specialized training session June 11 for state law enforcement officers and federal partners focused on campus extremism and antisemitic violence, citing recent attacks in Boulder, Colorado, and Washington, D.C., as evidence of an escalating threat.

The Birmingham event was organized in partnership with George Washington University and the Chabad of Alabama. Two retired FBI officials delivered the substantive sessions.

Retired FBI Special Agent and terrorism researcher Lara Burns led the first session, titled "Campus Extremism and Hamas in the United States." Burns drew on internal Hamas documents and evidence from federal prosecutions to outline the organization's activities in the U.S. and provide law enforcement with indicators for identifying and countering its influence on campuses.

The second session featured David Zimmerman, a retired FBI Supervisory Special Agent and current senior research fellow, who provided firsthand accounts from his deployment in Israel during and following the October 7, 2023, terrorist attacks. Zimmerman addressed operational challenges in countering global terrorism and its connections to domestic extremism.

Marshall framed the training as equipping campus administrators and local law enforcement with tools to identify key players, tactics, and narratives driving antisemitic networks.

The session is part of the attorney general's stated initiative to position Alabama as a leader in counterterrorism efforts and protection of the Jewish community.

headline: Barbara Thiel stellt Tätigkeitsbericht 2015-2016 vor | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
# Lower Saxony Data Protection Authority Releases Delayed GDPR Transition Report

Germany's Lower Saxony Data Protection Commissioner has published its 23rd activity report, covering 2015 and 2016, acknowledging the document arrived late due to the significant institutional demands of preparing for the EU General Data Protection Regulation.

The report's delayed release reflects the operational reality facing supervisory authorities during the GDPR transition period. When the regulation entered into force on May 25, 2016 — two years before its May 25, 2018 application date — authorities immediately redirected substantial staff resources toward implementation while continuing enforcement under existing law.

The Commissioner uses the report to address a recurring tension in data protection oversight: the conflict between public security demands and individual fundamental rights. The authority explicitly rejects the framing that data protection stands against security or economic growth, instead positioning supervisory bodies as necessary watchdogs ensuring political and commercial objectives do not disproportionately infringe on civil liberties.

The report also flags accelerating data volumes from rapid technological development as a sustained challenge requiring ongoing regulatory attention.

The document covers a period now significant in retrospect — the two-year window when authorities operated under legacy legal frameworks while building capacity for the GDPR's substantially expanded requirements, including new enforcement powers and higher penalty thresholds.

The 23rd activity report is available as a PDF download from the Lower Saxony Commissioner's office.

headline: Attorney General Marshall Supports President Trump’s Executive Order Keeping Men Out of Women’s Sports - Alabama Attorney General's Office
body:
# Alabama AG Marshall Joins Trump for Executive Order Banning Biological Males from Women's Sports

Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall attended the White House signing of President Donald Trump's executive order titled "Keep Men Out of Women's Sports" on February 5, 2025.

The executive order restricts biological males from participating in women's sports, reversing policies established under the Biden administration. Marshall praised the signing as addressing what he called "radical and damaging policies" that threatened opportunities for female athletes, including roster spots, scholarships, and other benefits tied to athletic participation.

Marshall has a record of legal action on related issues. He successfully challenged the Biden administration's expansion of Title IX regulations, which would have permitted biological males access to female-only spaces including locker rooms and bathrooms. He has also led multiple multistate coalitions filing amicus briefs in state and federal courts, including the U.S. Supreme Court, supporting state laws that limit girls' sports team participation to biological females.

The executive order represents a federal policy shift on a question that has largely been contested at the state level, where numerous states have enacted their own legislation restricting transgender athlete participation in women's sports. Marshall's office did not detail specific enforcement mechanisms tied to the order or identify which federal programs or funding streams the order affects.

headline: Alabama Attorney General Leads 23-State Brief Defending President Trump’s Executive Order Halting Federal Funding of Sex-Change Procedures for Children - Alabama Attorney General's Office
body:
# Alabama Leads 23-State Brief Defending Trump Order on Youth Gender Procedures

Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall led a 23-state amicus brief filed in federal court in Maryland defending President Trump's executive order directing federal agencies to stop funding gender transition procedures for minors, the office announced February 26.

The ACLU and Lambda Legal challenged the executive order in Maryland federal court. The coalition of state attorneys general filed the brief in support of the administration's position.

Marshall argues that medical guidelines used to justify such procedures were deliberately manipulated for political and legal purposes rather than grounded in scientific evidence. He claims his office uncovered evidence that leading medical standards of care were crafted specifically to support litigation and advance policy goals, independent of clinical evidence.

"Every single systematic evidence review shows the opposite" of what proponents claim, Marshall said, arguing the guidelines supporting gender procedures for minors have been debunked.

Marshall previously submitted a related amicus brief to the Supreme Court in October 2024 and has defended Alabama's Vulnerable Child Compassion and Protection Act in court.

Attorneys general from Alaska, Arkansas, Florida, Georgia, Idaho, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, North Dakota, Oklahoma, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Virginia, and West Virginia joined the brief, along with the Arizona State Legislature.

The case involves federal funding policy rather than state-level procedure bans.

headline: Barbara Thiel stellt Tätigkeitsbericht 2017 - 2018 vor | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
## German Data Protection Authority Fielded 8,000+ Consultations as GDPR Took Effect

A German data protection authority conducted more than 8,000 consultations in 2018 alone as organizations scrambled to comply with the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which entered into force on May 25, 2018 following a two-year transition period.

The surge in advisory requests reflects widespread unpreparedness among data processors, according to the authority's 24th Activity Report covering 2017-2018. Despite the regulator publishing guidance materials and hosting numerous events in 2017 to alert organizations to the new requirements, many data processors only recognized the scope of changes shortly before the regulation became applicable.

Staff handled consultation requests from companies, associations, organizations, and public bodies during what the report describes as a wave of inquiries that hit the authority in spring 2018.

The authority also participated in drafting new legislation during the reporting period, alongside handling cases under pre-GDPR law. The commissioner notes that data protection is frequently treated as an obstacle and bureaucratic burden rather than a prerequisite for individual freedom.

Looking ahead, the authority signals a shift in focus from advisory work back toward its supervisory mandate, with plans to conduct increased inspections and proactive audits not tied to specific complaints.

The report was issued during the commissioner's chairmanship year of the Conference of Independent Federal and State Data Protection Authorities.

headline: Barbara Thiel stellt Tätigkeitsbericht 2019 vor | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
## GDPR's First Full Year: 1,800 Complaints and Growing Legal Uncertainty, German Regulator Reports

A German data protection authority received more than 1,800 complaints from affected individuals and 820 breach notifications from data controllers during 2019, the first full calendar year of GDPR enforcement, according to the regulator's 2019 annual report.

The volume of complaints and mandatory breach reports under Article 33 GDPR has forced the authority into a predominantly reactive enforcement posture, limiting its capacity for unannounced inspections. The regulator describes this as "very regrettable," noting that proactive, unannounced audits consistently prove effective and are expected at the European level.

The report identifies persistent legal uncertainty as a structural challenge. Despite GDPR's dispute resolution mechanisms and the European Data Protection Board's role as final arbiter when national authorities disagree, numerous open legal questions remain without EU-wide consensus positions. The regulator expects courts to resolve many of these questions over the coming years.

Until judicial clarity emerges, the authority signals it will use enforcement practice and escalated sanctions to shape the law. The regulator explicitly commits to identifying questions requiring clarification and using sharper penalties to provide it.

On public awareness, the report credits GDPR's high-profile implementation period with building genuine citizen understanding of data rights and the complaints process — an effect the regulator characterizes as lasting rather than a short-term spike in attention.

headline: Tätigkeitsbericht 2024 | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
# German Privacy Watchdog Flags IT Market Concentration and Health Data Risks

North Rhine-Westphalia's state data protection authority has published its 30th Annual Activity Report for 2024, highlighting the growing dependence of public sector bodies on a small number of dominant IT vendors as a key regulatory concern.

The report identifies IT market concentration as a structural risk to data protection compliance across government institutions. Public authorities relying on a handful of major technology providers face reduced ability to enforce data protection requirements and maintain meaningful control over personal data processing.

The authority also flags digitalization in healthcare and public administration as priority areas requiring stronger privacy safeguards. Both sectors handle sensitive personal data at scale, making privacy-by-design principles critical to compliance.

Commissioner Denis Lehmkemper said data protection must be integrated throughout the entire digitalization process, from development through implementation. "Only in this way can trust and security be created and maintained long-term in the face of rapid innovation," Lehmkemper stated.

The report signals that regulators are increasingly concerned not only with individual compliance failures but with systemic dependencies that structurally weaken data protection across public institutions. Authorities procuring IT services from dominant market players may face greater scrutiny over their vendor management and contractual data protection arrangements.

The full 30th Activity Report is available as a PDF download from the authority's website.

headline: Meldung von Datenschutzbeauftragten | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
**Lower Saxony DPA Launches Online Portal for Data Protection Officer Registrations**

Germany's Lower Saxony data protection authority (LfD Niedersachsen) has launched a dedicated online portal allowing organizations to register their Data Protection Officers (DPOs) with the supervisory authority.

Under Article 37(7) of the GDPR, controllers and processors must both publish their DPO's contact details and notify their competent supervisory authority. The new portal, accessible at nds.dsb-meldung.de, gives organizations a streamlined way to meet this legal obligation.

The portal enables organizations to submit required DPO notifications, update existing registration data at any time, and receive electronic confirmation of successful registration for their own compliance documentation.

The LfD Niedersachsen's move digitizes a previously administrative process, reducing friction for the estimated thousands of organizations operating under its jurisdiction that are legally required to appoint a DPO. GDPR mandates DPO appointments for public authorities, organizations conducting large-scale systematic monitoring, and those processing sensitive data categories at scale.

The electronic confirmation function is particularly relevant for compliance purposes, giving organizations documentary proof that they have fulfilled their Article 37(7) notification requirement — a point that supervisory authorities can examine during audits.

The portal is now live and available to all controllers and processors subject to Lower Saxony's supervisory oversight.

headline: Attorney General Baker Joins With Nation's Attorneys General In Commemorating October As National Domestic Violence Awareness Month | Office of the Attorney GeneralLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia
body:
# Georgia AG Joins National Push for Domestic Violence Awareness Month

Georgia Attorney General Thurbert Baker joined the National Association of Attorneys General (NAAG) in October 2002 to mark National Domestic Violence Awareness Month, highlighting the crime's widespread impact and Georgia's pioneering legislative response.

Baker, co-convener of NAAG's Violence Against Women Committee, pointed to federal statistics showing nearly one-third of American women report physical or sexual abuse by a husband or boyfriend at some point in their lives. The same share of women murdered annually are killed by current or former partners. Approximately one million women report stalking each year, and 92% of domestic violence crimes are committed by men against women. Family violence costs the nation $5 to $10 billion annually in medical expenses.

A central focus of Baker's remarks was intergenerational violence. Research cited by Baker identifies a child's exposure to a father abusing the mother as the strongest risk factor for transmitting violent behavior across generations. In response, Baker successfully pushed legislation through the Georgia General Assembly making Georgia the first state in the nation to criminalize committing an act of domestic violence in the presence of a child.

Virginia Attorney General Jerry Kilgore, also a NAAG committee co-convener, called on employers to engage directly, noting domestic violence drives significant productivity losses, absenteeism, and elevated healthcare costs in workplaces nationwide.

Attorneys General pledged continued coordination with federal, state, and local agencies to implement enforcement strategies addressing domestic violence.

headline: 93-4 | Office of the Attorney GeneralLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia.gov logo
body:
# Georgia AG Opinion: Campus Weapons Ban Covers Colleges and Universities

Georgia's attorney general issued an unofficial opinion in July 1993 concluding that the state's prohibition on carrying weapons at schools under O.C.G.A. § 16-11-127.1 extends to colleges and universities.

The Eastern Judicial Circuit's district attorney had requested clarification after finding that the statute does not explicitly define "school." The opinion, prepared by staff attorney Kay Baker, applies two legal rationales to resolve the ambiguity.

First, the opinion relies on ordinary meaning. Citing *Curlee v. Mock Enterprises, Inc.* (1985), it holds that absent a statutory definition, words carry their common meaning — and "school," as commonly understood, includes colleges and universities.

Second, the opinion uses the statute's own exemptions as interpretive evidence. O.C.G.A. § 16-11-127.1(c)(3)(E) exempts campus police officers as defined under Title 20, Chapter 8 — a definition that explicitly covers officers employed at colleges and universities. The opinion reasons that exempting campus police from a prohibition that did not apply to their institutions would render that exemption meaningless surplusage, violating standard rules of statutory construction.

The opinion carries unofficial status, meaning it reflects legal analysis rather than binding authority. Nonetheless, it establishes that Georgia's weapons-on-school-grounds law applies across all educational levels, from K-12 through higher education.

headline: Carr Files Suit Against Biden Administration's Termination of Immigration Deportation Requirement | Office of the Attorney GeneralLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia.gov logo
body:
# Georgia Leads 21-State Lawsuit to Block Title 42 Termination

Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr filed suit April 14 in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Louisiana, joining 20 other states in seeking to block the Biden administration's revocation of Title 42, the CDC public health order allowing border officials to expel migrants during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The CDC announced the policy's termination on April 1, with an effective date of May 23, 2022. Since Title 42's introduction in March 2020, U.S. authorities have removed more than 1.7 million migrants under the order.

The lawsuit argues the revocation is arbitrary and capricious and violated federal rulemaking requirements by bypassing the notice-and-comment process. In February 2022, 55 percent of southern border encounters resulted in Title 42 expulsions. That month, CBP recorded 164,973 migrant encounters — up from 101,099 in February 2021 and 36,687 in February 2020.

The states cite fentanyl trafficking as a central concern. CBP data shows fentanyl seizures rose 1,066 percent in FY 2021. In Georgia, fentanyl-involved overdose deaths increased 106.2 percent between May 2020 and April 2021.

Joining Georgia are Alabama, Alaska, Arizona, Arkansas, Florida, Idaho, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, Ohio, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Tennessee, Utah, West Virginia, and Wyoming.

headline: Carr: Attorneys General Urge Congress to Pass Extension for CARES Act Funding | Office of the Attorney GeneralLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia.gov logo
body:
# 43 State AGs Urge Congress to Extend CARES Act Spending Deadline to End of 2021

Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr has joined a 43-state coalition urging Congress to extend the deadline for spending Coronavirus Aid, Relief and Economy (CARES) Act funds from December 30, 2020, through the end of 2021.

The coalition, led by Ohio AG Dave Yost and Iowa AG Tom Miller, includes attorneys general from 43 states, the District of Columbia, and five U.S. territories. The group sent its letter to Congress on November 30, 2020.

At issue is a restriction embedded in the CARES Act limiting fund use to expenses incurred between March 1 and December 30, 2020. The attorneys general argue that ongoing pandemic costs make the current deadline unworkable, preventing state and local governments from deploying the funds strategically.

Congress passed the CARES Act in March 2020, providing more than $2 trillion in economic stimulus to state and local governments, businesses, nonprofits, and families to offset pandemic-related costs and economic disruption.

The coalition is urging passage of any one of several pending legislative measures that would grant states additional time to utilize existing COVID-relief resources. No new funding is being requested — only an extension of the spending window for already-allocated dollars.

"We believe this is a sensible solution to continue this much needed relief for our communities," Carr said.

headline: Pink Plumber to Pay $102,000 in Restitution and Penalties to Settle Claims of Deceptive Advertising | Office of the Attorney GeneralLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia.gov logo
body:
**Georgia Attorney General Settles Deceptive Advertising Case Against Pink Plumber for $102,190**

Georgia Attorney General Sam Olens has reached a settlement with Aames Heating and Plumbing, Inc., doing business as The Pink Plumber, and its owner Geoffrey Gillon over violations of Georgia's Fair Business Practices Act.

The state alleged the Atlanta-area plumbing company engaged in multiple deceptive practices targeting consumers seeking septic services. Specifically, the company advertised septic pumping services at $195 or less but consistently charged higher prices. The company also represented that "hydro jetting" and additive treatments were necessary and beneficial, despite regulatory agencies characterizing those services and products as providing questionable benefit at best and potentially damaging septic systems. Additionally, the company allowed uncertified employees to perform state-regulated services.

Under the Assurance of Voluntary Compliance signed by both parties, The Pink Plumber and Gillon must pay $27,190.14 in consumer restitution and $75,000 in penalties and fees to the state. Non-compliance triggers an additional $125,000 penalty. The company is also prohibited from making false or misleading claims about pricing and service effectiveness and must ensure only state-certified technicians perform regulated services.

The Georgia Department of Public Health's Environmental Health Division assisted in the investigation.

"We will not tolerate bait and switch tactics such as these," Olens said in the announcement.

headline: Carr Prosecutes Former Chief Magistrate Judge in Pickens County for Public Corruption | Office of the Attorney GeneralLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia.gov logo
body:
# Georgia Judge Convicted of 49 Counts Including Racketeering for Stealing Public Funds

Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr announced May 27, 2021 that former Pickens County Chief Magistrate Judge William "Allen" Wigington was convicted on 49 counts — 44 felonies and 5 misdemeanors — and sentenced to 15 years, with 5 years in custody and the remainder on probation.

Wigington's convictions include racketeering, fourth-degree forgery, theft by taking, unauthorized use of a financial transaction card, and violation of oath of office.

The case began when a Masonic Lodge in Jasper, Georgia — where Wigington served as treasurer — discovered he had paid his personal Chase credit card bill using the Lodge's bank account. When confronted, Wigington fabricated a story about the account being hacked and produced forged Chase Bank reimbursement checks to support the lie. A GBI subpoena to Chase confirmed the checks were forgeries.

Investigators also found Wigington used his county-issued credit card to make thousands of dollars in personal Amazon purchases, double-billed Pickens County for travel reimbursements, and stole $200 designated to buy a suit for a child whose family could not afford one.

The GBI and Pickens County Sheriff's Office conducted the joint investigation. Wigington had served as Chief Magistrate Judge since May 2011 and resigned in February 2020 amid the charges.

"The theft of taxpayer funds will not go unpunished," Carr said.

headline: Meldung von Datenschutzverletzungen nach Artikel 33 DS-GVO | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
body:
## GDPR Data Breach Reporting: 72-Hour Notification Rule Explained

Data controllers must report personal data breaches to their competent supervisory authority immediately and within 72 hours of becoming aware of an incident, under Article 33 of the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

The requirement applies to controllers as defined under Article 4(7) GDPR. Reporting is not required when a breach is unlikely to result in any risk or poses only a low risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals.

Even when no formal report is required, controllers still face a mandatory documentation obligation under Article 33(5) GDPR. This internal record must be maintained to demonstrate compliance with the accountability principle established in Article 5(2) GDPR.

Processors — organizations handling data on behalf of a controller — carry a separate obligation. When a processor becomes aware of a breach, it must notify the controller immediately, though the processor itself is not responsible for filing the supervisory authority report.

Regulators point organizations to EDSA Guidelines 9/2022 on data breaches for detailed compliance guidance.

The rules create a two-track system: external notification to authorities for higher-risk incidents, and internal documentation for all incidents regardless of risk level. Individuals who are not themselves controllers but wish to report unlawful data handling are directed to use a separate online complaint form rather than the breach notification channel.

headline: Medicaid Fraud and Patient Protection Division | Office of the Attorney GeneralLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia.gov logo
body:
# Georgia Medicaid Fraud Division Secures Convictions, Indictments in Multi-Million Dollar Cases

Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr's Medicaid Fraud and Patient Protection Division has secured a conviction and multiple indictments in cases totaling over $8 million in fraudulent Medicaid billing, according to recent announcements from the Georgia Department of Law.

A Warner Robins woman was convicted on January 13, 2026, of defrauding Georgia Medicaid of $5.4 million. Separately, owners of a behavioral health facility in DeKalb County were indicted in December 2025 on Medicaid fraud charges totaling over $1.4 million. A third case, filed in June 2025, charged the owner of a durable medical equipment company by federal indictment in a separate $5.4 million fraud scheme.

The division operates under the Georgia Department of Law following a 2011 consolidation ordered by executive action. Prior to that restructuring, Medicaid fraud enforcement was split across three agencies — the Department of Law, the Georgia Bureau of Investigations, and the Department of Audits.

The division investigates fraud, abuse, neglect, and exploitation involving Georgia's Medicaid program and accepts complaints through its online reporting form. The office operates Monday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.

Georgians who suspect Medicaid fraud can file a report directly with the Medicaid Fraud and Patient Protection Division through the Georgia Department of Law's official website.

headline: Press ReleasesLanguage iconGeorgia.gov logoGeorgia.gov logo
body:
# Georgia AG's Office Pursues Human Trafficking, Vape Sales, Drug Enforcement in April Actions

Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr's office has taken action across multiple enforcement areas in April 2026, targeting human trafficking, illegal vape products, and dangerous drugs.

The most recent action, announced April 24, saw Carr's Human Trafficking Prosecution Unit recover 11 missing children in Georgia through an operation called "Coast to Coast." Days earlier, on April 13, the office charged a McDuffie County man with trafficking an underage female.

On the consumer protection front, Carr on April 22 urged payment processors to halt transactions tied to illegal Chinese-manufactured vapes, while also warning Georgians about price gouging and scams connected to wildfires. The office did not specify which payment processors received the outreach.

On April 16, Carr announced efforts to combat the spread of what the office described as a deadly tranquilizer drug, and on April 15, he pushed for a federal rule that would increase transparency in prescription drug pricing.

The office also announced on April 23 that three individuals were indicted for targeting a contractor at a training center in Cobb County.

On April 7, Carr joined a 25-state coalition filing an amicus brief against noncitizen voting, and on April 2, he launched the "Eyes on the Road" campaign to help locate missing children in Georgia.
